q: Design an image-loading and caching library for iOS (memory + disk cache, cancellation, prefetch, decoding).
a: I'd start with a single entry point that takes a URL and a target view, returning a cancellable task. Under the hood there's a two-tier cache: an in-memory `NSCache` for decoded bitmaps and a disk cache for the original compressed bytes, fronted by a `URLSession` for network fetches. Decoding happens off the main thread and I force-decode plus downsample to the view's pixel size so we never hand the render server a huge image. The whole thing is keyed by a normalized cache key so identical requests coalesce into one in-flight download.
d: Decompose into a Loader (orchestration), a Cache layer (memory + disk), a Downloader (network + request coalescing), a Decoder/Processor pipeline, and a small task handle for cancellation. Data flow: check memory cache for a decoded image, then disk for raw bytes (decode off-main), then network. The memory tier should store decoded `UIImage`/`CGImage` because decoding is the expensive step; the disk tier stores original bytes so we can re-decode at different sizes. Concurrency: a dedicated decode queue with bounded width, and a dictionary of in-flight tasks under a lock so N requests for the same URL share one download and one decode. Cancellation must propagate from the cell-reuse point down to the `URLSessionTask`, and for cell reuse you tag the request so a recycled cell ignores a late completion. Downsampling via `CGImageSourceCreateThumbnailAtIndex` is the key scaling decision — memory is dominated by decoded pixels, not file size. Failure handling: respect HTTP cache headers (`ETag`, `Cache-Control`), cap retries, and never cache error responses as if they were images. Interviewers probe for the decode-off-main-thread insight, the coalescing of duplicate requests, and correct cancellation on fast scrolling.
f: Trap: "Why not just cache decoded images on disk to skip decoding?" Decoded bitmaps are huge (width*height*4 bytes) and balloon disk usage; you'd also lose the ability to re-downsample for different display sizes. Store compressed originals on disk and only cache decoded images in memory where the cost/benefit favors speed over space.

q: Design an offline-first feed (sync, conflict resolution, optimistic updates).
a: The UI reads exclusively from a local store — Core Data or SQLite — so the feed always renders instantly, online or not. A sync engine reconciles that store with the server using delta sync keyed on a cursor or updated-at watermark, and writes are recorded as local mutations applied optimistically before the network round-trip. On conflict I default to server-authoritative for content I don't own and last-write-wins with a version token for content I do, surfacing a rollback if the server rejects. The view layer observes the store via `NSFetchedResultsController` or a publisher, so any sync update animates in automatically.
d: Key components: a local persistent store as the single source of truth, a read path (UI binds to the store, never to the network), a write path (an outbox/mutation queue), and a sync coordinator handling pull (delta) and push (replay outbox). Optimistic updates mean each user action immediately mutates the store and enqueues a pending operation with a client-generated ID for idempotency. Conflict resolution depends on data shape: counters want commutative merges, content fields want LWW with a server version or `ETag`, and ordering wants server timestamps to avoid clock skew. Scaling: delta sync transfers only changes since the last cursor; pagination plus the watermark keeps payloads bounded. Failure handling: the outbox must survive app kills, retry with backoff, and dedup via idempotency keys so a replayed mutation doesn't double-post. Edge cases include tombstones for deletes (so a re-pull doesn't resurrect deleted items), reordering of dependent mutations, and reconciling an optimistic item's temporary ID with the server-assigned one. Interviewers probe how you keep the UI consistent during a failed optimistic write and how you avoid duplicate or lost mutations across process death.
f: Trap: "Last-write-wins is simple — just use it everywhere." LWW silently drops concurrent edits and is wrong for counters or collaborative text; for those you need commutative/CRDT merges or operation-based sync. Choose the strategy per field, not globally.

q: Design a chat app (real-time transport, ordering, delivery/read receipts, offline queue).
a: I'd use a persistent WebSocket for low-latency bidirectional messaging, with a REST fallback for history and a reconnect-and-catch-up flow. Messages get a client-generated ID and a server-assigned monotonic sequence number, so I order by server sequence and dedupe by client ID. Outgoing messages go through a local send queue persisted to disk, shown immediately as 'sending' and reconciled to 'sent/delivered/read' as acks arrive. Receipts are just lightweight events on the same channel that update per-message state in the local store.
d: Components: a transport layer (WebSocket with heartbeat/ping and exponential reconnect), a message store (local DB as source of truth), a send queue/outbox, and a sync-on-reconnect mechanism. Ordering is the crux: never trust client clocks — use a server-assigned sequence per conversation and on reconnect request everything after the last seen sequence to fill gaps. Delivery state machine per message: composing -> queued -> sent (server ack) -> delivered (recipient device ack) -> read (recipient read receipt). Offline: the outbox persists pending sends and replays on reconnect; idempotency via client message ID prevents duplicates if an ack was lost. Concurrency: serialize writes to the store per conversation to keep ordering deterministic. Scaling concerns: large conversations need windowed pagination, and fan-out of receipts must be batched to avoid chatter. Failure modes: dropped socket mid-send (replay and dedupe), out-of-order delivery (sort by sequence), and clock skew (ignore client timestamps for ordering). Interviewers probe how you detect and fill gaps after reconnect and how you guarantee exactly-once display despite at-least-once delivery.
f: Trap: "Order messages by the timestamp the sender stamped." Client clocks drift and can be set wrong, producing scrambled or future-dated messages; order by a server-assigned monotonic sequence and keep the client time only as a display hint.

q: Design an analytics SDK (batching, persistence, flush policy, privacy).
a: Events are enqueued through a non-blocking API that immediately persists them to a local buffer so nothing is lost on crash, then a background flusher batches them to the server. Flush triggers on three conditions: batch size threshold, a time interval, and app-background transition, all with exponential backoff on failure. I attach a stable but resettable device/install ID and scrub PII at the boundary, honoring consent and the system tracking authorization before anything leaves the device. The whole pipeline runs off the main thread on a serial queue so instrumentation never janks the UI.
d: Decompose into a public track() API, an in-memory queue, a durable store (SQLite or an append log), a batching/flush scheduler, and a network uploader. The track() call must be cheap and thread-safe: hop to a serial queue, enrich with context (session, app version, timestamp), and append to storage. Flush policy balances battery/network against freshness — size + interval + lifecycle events, with `URLSession` background or just on-foreground uploads. Persistence guarantees at-least-once delivery, so the server must dedupe via per-event UUIDs; only delete local events after a confirmed 2xx. Backoff and a max-queue cap prevent a flaky network from unbounded growth — drop oldest or sample under pressure. Privacy is first-class: consent gating, PII scrubbing, IP truncation, and respecting ATT/opt-out, with a kill switch via remote config. Testability: inject the clock, the store, and the network so you can assert batching boundaries deterministically. Interviewers probe data-loss guarantees across crashes, ordering, and how you bound memory/disk under sustained offline use.
f: Trap: "Just keep events in memory and send them when convenient." An app kill or crash loses all buffered events and you lose exactly the sessions where something went wrong; persist before acknowledging and delete only after a confirmed upload.

q: Design a networking layer (request abstraction, retry/backoff, auth token refresh, interceptors).
a: At the core is a request abstraction — an endpoint describing path, method, body, and an associated decoded response type — sent through a client built on `URLSession`. Around it I add a chain of interceptors for cross-cutting concerns: auth injection, logging, retry, and token refresh. Token refresh is handled by a single-flight actor so that when many requests hit a 401 at once, only one refresh runs and the rest await its result. Retries use exponential backoff with jitter and are limited to idempotent or explicitly-safe requests.
d: Components: an Endpoint/Request type (often generic over a `Decodable` response), a Client that builds `URLRequest`s and runs them, an interceptor pipeline (adapt outgoing, retry on failure), and an auth manager. The interceptor pattern keeps concerns composable: an adapter signs requests and attaches the bearer token; a retrier decides whether a failure is retryable and how long to wait. Token refresh is the subtle part — concurrent 401s must coalesce into one refresh (an actor or a serialized task holding a single in-flight refresh future), then replay the queued requests with the new token; if refresh fails, fail them all and trigger logout. Backoff with jitter avoids thundering-herd retries; cap attempts and only retry idempotent methods or those marked safe. Error modeling should distinguish transport errors, HTTP status errors, and decoding errors so callers react appropriately. Testability comes from protocol-abstracting the session (or using `URLProtocol` stubs) and injecting the clock for backoff. Scaling: connection reuse via one shared session, request prioritization, and cancellation propagation. Interviewers probe the concurrent-refresh race and how you avoid retrying a non-idempotent POST.
f: Trap: "Refresh the token inline in each request that gets a 401." Ten concurrent 401s would fire ten refreshes, racing and invalidating each other's tokens; serialize refresh through a single-flight actor and have the other requests await the one result.

q: Design pagination (cursor vs offset, prefetch, dedup).
a: I prefer cursor-based pagination over offset because cursors stay stable when items are inserted or deleted between fetches, avoiding skips and duplicates. The client holds the next-page cursor, requests when the user nears the end of the list, and merges results into a local model deduped by stable item ID. I trigger prefetch a screen or two ahead using the collection view's prefetch APIs so scrolling feels seamless. Loading, error, and end-of-list states are explicit so the UI can show spinners or retry affordances.
d: Decompose into a Pager (holds cursor + page state), a data source merging pages, and a trigger tied to scroll position or `UICollectionViewDataSourcePrefetching`. Offset pagination is simple and supports jumping to a page, but on a mutating dataset rows shift and you get duplicates or gaps; cursor (keyset) pagination encodes 'after this sort key/id' and is stable and index-friendly on the backend. Dedup is essential because overlapping pages or optimistic inserts can repeat items — key your in-memory set by server ID and ignore re-arrivals, or upsert. State management needs an explicit machine: idle, loading-first, loaded, loading-next, end, error — and you must guard against firing concurrent next-page requests. Concurrency: one in-flight page request at a time, with cancellation if the user navigates away. Edge cases: empty pages, the last page (no cursor returned), pull-to-refresh resetting the cursor, and reconciling refreshed head with already-loaded tail. Interviewers probe why cursors beat offset on live data and how you prevent duplicate-page fetches during fast scroll.
f: Trap: "Offset pagination is fine, just use LIMIT/OFFSET." On a feed where items are added at the head, OFFSET makes page 2 overlap page 1 (duplicates) or skip rows; use a stable cursor (keyset) on the sort key so paging is insertion-safe.

q: Design a generic sync engine (last-write-wins vs CRDT vs operational, delta sync).
a: I'd model sync as a bidirectional reconciliation between a local store and the server, using delta sync — each side tracks a version vector or a server cursor and exchanges only changes since the last sync point. The conflict strategy is pluggable per entity: last-write-wins with a logical timestamp for simple fields, CRDTs for counters and sets that must merge commutatively, and operational transforms for collaborative ordered text. Local mutations queue in an outbox with idempotency keys, and deletes use tombstones so they propagate instead of resurrecting. The engine is deterministic and replay-safe so the same set of operations always converges to the same state.
d: Core pieces: change tracking (per-record version/cursor), a pull pipeline (apply remote deltas), a push pipeline (replay local ops), and a merge/conflict resolver. Delta sync minimizes payload — server returns records changed after a watermark; the client must handle pagination of deltas and a 'reset/full-sync' signal when the watermark is too old. LWW is cheapest but lossy under concurrency; CRDTs guarantee convergence without coordination but cost metadata and complexity; OT needs a central server to transform operations and is heavy. Choose by data semantics, not preference. Idempotency keys make at-least-once delivery safe; tombstones plus a retention window handle deletes. Failure modes: partial sync interrupted mid-batch (apply atomically or make replay idempotent), clock skew (use logical clocks/Lamport timestamps, not wall time), and divergence detection (checksums/version vectors). Scaling: batch deltas, compress, and coalesce redundant ops in the outbox. Testability: property-test convergence — random op orderings must reach the same final state. Interviewers probe convergence guarantees and when CRDTs are overkill.
f: Trap: "Use wall-clock timestamps to decide the winner in LWW." Device clocks disagree and can be wrong, so a stale device can clobber newer data; use logical/Lamport clocks or server-assigned versions to order events causally.

q: Design a push notification pipeline (APNs, silent pushes, dedup, deep-linking).
a: On the client I register for remote notifications, upload the APNs device token to my backend keyed by user and install, and handle token rotation. The backend sends through APNs with a payload that carries a stable notification ID, a deep-link target, and either visible content or the content-available flag for silent pushes. On receipt I dedupe by the notification ID, do a quick background fetch for silent pushes within the time budget, and route the deep link through the same central router the app uses for universal links. Foreground vs background presentation is handled explicitly so we don't double-show.
d: Components: client registration + token management, a backend notification service, the APNs gateway, and a client-side handler/router. Token lifecycle matters — tokens change on reinstall/restore, so re-upload on every launch and let the server dedupe stale tokens. Silent pushes (content-available: 1) are best-effort and rate-limited by iOS, run in a ~30s background window, and must finish quickly or iOS throttles you; never rely on them for guaranteed delivery. Dedup is needed because APNs is best-effort-at-least-once and a user may have multiple devices — carry a server-generated notification ID and ignore repeats, also coalescing with `apns-collapse-id`. Deep-linking should funnel through one router so a tap from a notification, a universal link, and an in-app navigation all resolve identically, with a fallback if the target is unavailable. Failure handling: handle the not-authorized state, provisional auth, and the case where the app is killed (only a tap relaunch gives you the payload). Testability: abstract the router and payload parser. Interviewers probe silent-push reliability limits and consistent deep-link routing across entry points.
f: Trap: "Use silent pushes to reliably keep data in sync in the background." iOS budgets and throttles `content-available` pushes and may drop them under low power or heavy usage, so they're best-effort only; treat them as an optimization and reconcile authoritatively on next foreground.

q: Design a feature-flag / remote-config system (caching, defaults, rollout, A/B).
a: The client ships with hardcoded safe defaults, fetches a config bundle on launch (and periodically), caches it to disk, and reads flags synchronously from that cache so evaluation never blocks. Each flag resolves through a precedence chain: local override for QA, then cached remote value, then the built-in default. Rollouts and A/B assignment are computed by hashing a stable user/install ID into buckets so a user stays in the same variant deterministically. Changes apply on next launch or via a live publisher, and there's a kill-switch path for risky features.
d: Components: a flag registry with typed accessors and defaults, a fetcher, a disk cache, an evaluator, and an exposure/analytics hook. Defaults are non-negotiable — the app must work offline and on first launch before any fetch completes, so reads are synchronous against cache-or-default. Rollout is deterministic bucketing: hash(userID + flagKey) mod 100 against a percentage so re-evaluation is stable and consistent across sessions and devices for the same user. A/B requires logging an exposure event when a variant is actually read (not when fetched) so analysis only counts users who hit the code path. Caching strategy: fetch in background, never block UI, apply atomically to avoid mixed-state config, and consider whether flags update mid-session or only at launch (launch-only is safer for consistency). Failure handling: stale cache is better than failing; validate/schema-check fetched config and fall back if malformed. Kill switches must be reachable even when other systems fail. Testability: inject a flag provider so tests force any combination. Interviewers probe deterministic bucketing and when exposure is logged.
f: Trap: "Read the flag value from the network each time it's checked." That blocks the UI, fails offline, and can change behavior mid-session causing inconsistent state; read synchronously from a cached snapshot with built-in defaults and refresh out of band.

q: Design a persistence strategy (choosing Core Data vs SQLite vs files vs SwiftData).
a: I'd pick based on the data's shape, query needs, and concurrency. For a rich object graph with relationships, change observation, and undo, Core Data or SwiftData earns its complexity; for high-volume, query-heavy, or migration-sensitive data I'd reach for SQLite (often via GRDB) for control and performance; for blobs and large media, flat files on disk referenced from the database; and for tiny preferences, `UserDefaults` or the keychain for secrets. The deciding factors are query complexity, write volume, threading model, and how much migration risk I can tolerate.
d: Decompose the decision: object graph + relationships + faulting/observation favors Core Data/SwiftData; raw SQL power, predictable performance, and explicit migrations favor SQLite/GRDB; opaque large binaries belong on the filesystem with paths stored in the DB to keep rows small; ephemeral or simple key-values go to `UserDefaults`; credentials go to the keychain. Concurrency differs sharply — Core Data uses managed object contexts confined to queues with a background context for writes merging into the view context, while GRDB offers a clean reader/writer model with WAL for concurrent reads. Migration is a major axis: Core Data lightweight migration handles additive changes but heavy schema changes need mapping models; SQLite migrations are explicit and versioned, which some teams prefer for predictability. SwiftData is ergonomic and SwiftUI-native but younger, with less escape-hatch control. Failure handling: corruption recovery, atomic writes for files, and backup exclusion flags for caches. Testability: abstract behind a repository protocol so the store is swappable and tests use in-memory configurations. Interviewers probe the threading model and migration story, not just CRUD.
f: Trap: "Store large images directly as a binary attribute so it's all in one place." Large blobs bloat the store, hurt fetch performance, and complicate faulting; keep big binaries as files on disk (or external storage / allowsExternalBinaryDataStorage) and store only the reference.

q: Design a media/video player and streaming cache.
a: I'd build on `AVPlayer` with `AVPlayerItem`s, using HLS for adaptive bitrate so playback adjusts to bandwidth automatically. For caching I'd intercept resource loading via `AVAssetResourceLoaderDelegate` to serve and store byte ranges, or use `AVAssetDownloadTask` for proper offline HLS downloads with DRM-friendly storage. The player layer is decoupled behind a view-model that exposes state — buffering, playing, paused, failed — and manages prefetching the next item in a playlist. Audio session, background playback, and Now Playing info are configured up front.
d: Components: a playback engine wrapping `AVPlayer`/`AVPlayerItem`, a state machine (idle/loading/buffering/playing/paused/ended/failed observed via KVO on item status and timeControlStatus), a caching/resource-loader layer, and a playlist/prefetch coordinator. For progressive MP4 you can cache via a custom `AVAssetResourceLoaderDelegate` that proxies requests, stores ranges to disk, and replays them — but HLS offline is better served by `AVAssetDownloadTask` which handles the manifest and segments correctly. Adaptive bitrate (HLS) is the right call for variable networks; you set preferred peak bitrate to cap data use. Concurrency: observe player on the main thread but do disk IO off it; preroll the next item to avoid gaps. Failure handling: stalls (monitor `playbackBufferEmpty`/`likelyToKeepUp`), network drops (retry/resume), and expired DRM licenses. Memory: release `AVPlayerItem`s you're not using and avoid holding many decoded frames. Edge cases: interruptions (calls), route changes (headphones unplugged), and Picture-in-Picture. Testability: abstract the player behind a protocol so the view-model is unit-testable without real media. Interviewers probe HLS vs progressive caching and stall recovery.
f: Trap: "Just cache the whole HLS stream by saving the .m3u8 and reusing it." HLS is a manifest pointing at many segment files and possibly DRM keys, so saving the playlist text alone caches nothing playable offline; use `AVAssetDownloadTask` which persists the segments and manifest into a managed asset.

q: Design a file download manager (background downloads, resume, concurrency limits).
a: I'd use a `URLSession` configured for background transfers so downloads continue after the app is suspended and the system relaunches us on completion. Each download is tracked with persisted metadata — URL, destination, resume data, state — so we can resume after interruption or app kill. A scheduler enforces a max concurrency limit and a priority queue, starting queued items as slots free up. On failure I capture the resume data and retry with backoff, and on success I atomically move the temp file to its final location.
d: Components: a session manager (background `URLSessionConfiguration`), a persistent task registry, a scheduler enforcing concurrency/priority, and a delegate handling progress, completion, and the background-events callback. Background sessions require implementing the app-delegate handler that stores the completion handler and calls it when the session reports finished events, plus handling relaunch where you must reattach to the existing session by identifier. Resume relies on `cancel(byProducingResumeData:)` and storing that blob; servers must support range requests (`Accept-Ranges`/`ETag`) or you restart from zero. Concurrency limiting is a scheduler concern, not a session config — keep an in-flight count and a waiting queue, respecting priorities and pausing on cellular if the user opted out. Persistence is mandatory because the app can be killed mid-download; store enough to rebuild state on launch. Failure handling: validate `ETag`/checksum after download (partial/corrupt files), atomic rename to avoid exposing half-written files, and dedup concurrent requests for the same URL. Edge cases: disk full, expired URLs, and the system reassigning tasks on relaunch. Interviewers probe background-session reattachment and correct resume-data handling.
f: Trap: "Limit concurrency with httpMaximumConnectionsPerHost." That caps connections to one host, not your overall queue, and doesn't give priority control across hosts; enforce a real scheduler with an in-flight counter and a waiting queue.

q: Design an in-app purchase / receipt validation flow.
a: I'd use StoreKit 2, which gives signed, server-verified transactions via `Transaction.currentEntitlements` and JWS payloads, removing most of the legacy receipt-parsing pain. The flow is: fetch products, present purchase, await the transaction, verify its signature, unlock content, then call `finish()` only after the entitlement is durably granted. For server-backed entitlements I validate with App Store Server APIs or App Store Server Notifications so subscription state stays authoritative on the backend. I always listen for transaction updates on launch to catch purchases made on other devices or interrupted flows.
d: Components: a product catalog loader, a purchase coordinator, a verification step, an entitlement store, and a transaction-updates listener. With StoreKit 2 each transaction is JWS-signed; verify it (locally or, better, server-side) before granting anything to prevent spoofing. Critically, you start the updates listener at app launch — purchases can complete asynchronously, on another device via Family Sharing, or after an interrupted flow (Ask to Buy, billing retry), and you must process those. Don't call `finish()` until the entitlement is recorded durably, or you'll lose the purchase if the app dies between grant and finish. For subscriptions, the backend is the source of truth: consume App Store Server Notifications v2 for renewals, cancellations, refunds, and grace periods rather than polling. Failure handling: pending/deferred transactions, refunds (revoke access), and offline grants reconciled on reconnect. Security: never trust client-only state for paid content; verify signatures and prefer server validation. Testability: StoreKit configuration files and the local testing environment simulate purchases and renewals. Interviewers probe verifying before granting and handling transactions that arrive asynchronously.
f: Trap: "Call transaction.finish() as soon as the purchase returns, then unlock." If the app crashes after finish but before persisting the entitlement, the purchase is gone and unrecoverable; persist/grant the entitlement first and only then finish the transaction.

q: Design a search-as-you-type system (debounce, cancellation, ranking).
a: As the user types I debounce input by a few hundred milliseconds so we don't fire a request per keystroke, and I cancel any in-flight request when a newer query arrives. Results merge local (already-cached or on-device) matches for instant feedback with remote results when they arrive, ranked by relevance. I guard against out-of-order responses by tagging each request with its query and dropping stale completions. The UI shows loading, empty, and error states distinctly so the experience stays predictable.
d: Components: an input pipeline (debounce + dedupe identical queries), a query coordinator with cancellation, a local index for instant matches, a remote search client, and a ranking/merge step. Debounce trades latency for fewer requests; pair it with cancellation so a stale request never overwrites fresh results. Out-of-order responses are the classic bug — even with cancellation, a slow earlier response can land after a faster later one, so stamp results with the query and discard mismatches. Ranking blends signals: prefix/exact match weight, recency, popularity, and personalization; on-device you might use simple scoring while the server does the heavy ranking. Concurrency: a serial coordinator that holds the latest query and a single in-flight task, cancelling the prior. Caching recent queries' results avoids re-fetching when the user backspaces. Failure handling: empty-query reset, network errors falling back to local results, and rate-limiting the backend. Performance: keep the local index small and search off the main thread. Testability: inject the clock for debounce and a fake search client. Interviewers probe handling stale/out-of-order responses and the debounce-vs-cancel interaction.
f: Trap: "Debouncing alone prevents stale results from showing." Debounce only reduces request frequency; a debounced request can still return after a newer one, so you also need cancellation plus query-tagging to drop late responses.

q: Design an offline maps / tile cache.
a: Maps render as a grid of tiles addressed by zoom/x/y, so I'd cache tiles on disk keyed by that coordinate plus style version, with an LRU eviction policy and a size cap. A tile provider checks the disk cache first, then fetches missing tiles from the network and stores them, while a region-download feature lets users pre-fetch all tiles for a bounding box at chosen zoom levels for true offline use. Tile decoding and IO happen off the main thread, and the visible viewport drives prefetch of adjacent tiles. Cache invalidation keys on a style/version token so map updates don't serve stale tiles.
d: Components: a tile coordinate model (z/x/y), a tile provider (cache-then-network), a disk cache with LRU + byte budget, a region-prefetch downloader, and a renderer feeding from the provider. The cache key must include style and version so a server-side map refresh invalidates cleanly rather than showing mismatched tiles. Prefetch strategy: load the visible tiles plus a one-tile ring and the next zoom level to make pan/zoom smooth. Region downloads enumerate every tile in a bounding box across a zoom range — counts grow ~4x per zoom level, so you cap zoom and warn on size. Concurrency: bounded parallel fetches with prioritization toward the viewport, cancelling tiles scrolled off-screen. Storage: huge tile counts make a single SQLite/MBTiles container often better than millions of small files (fewer inodes, atomic, easier eviction). Failure handling: missing tiles render a placeholder, partial region downloads resume, and disk pressure triggers eviction of least-recently-used tiles. Edge cases: device storage limits, retina vs non-retina tile sets, and date-line/pole wrapping. Interviewers probe the tile-count explosion and cache-invalidation keying.
f: Trap: "Store each tile as its own file on disk." Millions of tiny files exhaust inodes, slow enumeration/eviction, and fragment storage; pack tiles into a container like SQLite/MBTiles for atomicity and efficient LRU management.

q: Design an undo/redo system.
a: I'd model every user action as a reversible command — the Command pattern — pushing each onto an undo stack and clearing the redo stack on a new action. Undo pops a command, applies its inverse, and pushes it to the redo stack; redo does the reverse. For text and document editing I'd often use the Memento approach of snapshotting state, or `UndoManager` which already implements grouped, named undo on iOS. The key is capturing enough information to reverse an action exactly, and grouping related changes so one undo feels like one logical step.
d: Two main designs: command-based (store do/undo operations, memory-efficient, but each op must be cleanly invertible) and snapshot/memento-based (store state copies, simple and robust but memory-heavy — mitigate with diffs or copy-on-write). `UndoManager` is the idiomatic iOS path: register undo with a target/closure, group changes via begin/end grouping, and get coalescing and naming for free. Grouping is essential — a single user gesture may produce many model mutations that should undo together. Concurrency: undo state is UI state and should be touched on the main actor; if the model mutates from background work, route changes through a serialized command applier. Edge cases: a new action after undo must invalidate the redo branch; actions that aren't reversible (network side effects) shouldn't go on the stack or need compensating actions; and you must cap stack size to bound memory. Testability: pure command objects with do/undo are trivially unit-testable. Interviewers probe invertibility (some operations lose information unless you capture the prior value) and how you group multi-step edits into one undo.
f: Trap: "Just store the new value for each change to undo it." To reverse a change you need the previous value, not the new one; capture the prior state (or a true inverse operation), otherwise undo can't restore what was overwritten.

q: Design a logging and crash-reporting system.
a: For logging I'd use a leveled facade over `os_log`/`Logger` so messages are categorized, privacy-redacted, and cheap when disabled, with optional buffering to disk for later upload. Crash reporting needs a signal/exception handler that captures the stack at crash time, persists a minimal report, and uploads it on next launch — never during the crash itself. Reports carry symbolicated stacks (via dSYMs server-side), device/app context, and breadcrumbs of recent log events for diagnosis. Everything respects privacy: redact PII and gate uploads on consent.
d: Components: a logging facade (levels, categories, redaction), a ring buffer of recent breadcrumbs, a crash handler (mach exception / signal handlers and an uncaught-exception handler), a persistence layer, and an uploader. Crucial constraint: inside a crash handler you can only do async-signal-safe work — write a compact report to a pre-allocated buffer/file, do not allocate or call most Foundation APIs, and upload on the next clean launch. Symbolication happens server-side using uploaded dSYMs, so the client sends raw addresses plus binary images. Breadcrumbs (recent log lines, navigation, network events) dramatically aid debugging and should be captured in a bounded in-memory ring also flushed to disk. Logging performance: avoid string interpolation when the level is disabled, and use `os_log`'s privacy specifiers to keep user data out of system logs. Failure handling: dedupe identical crashes, throttle uploads, and bound disk usage. Privacy/consent gating and a kill switch are mandatory. Testability: inject the persistence and upload layers; simulate signals in tests cautiously. Interviewers probe async-signal-safety in the crash path and why you upload on next launch, not during the crash.
f: Trap: "In the crash handler, format a nice report and POST it immediately." The process is in an undefined state and most allocation/networking calls aren't async-signal-safe, so you'll likely deadlock or corrupt the report; write minimal data with signal-safe calls and upload on the next launch.

q: Design a deep-linking / universal links router.
a: I'd funnel every entry point — universal links, custom URL schemes, push taps, Spotlight, and shortcuts — through one central router that parses a URL into a typed route and resolves it to a navigation action. The router maps path patterns to handlers, validates parameters, and falls back gracefully when a target is unknown or the user isn't authenticated. Navigation is expressed declaratively so the router can build the full screen stack, not just push one view. Universal links are configured with an `apple-app-site-association` file and associated-domains entitlement.
d: Components: a URL parser, a route model (typed enum of destinations), a registry mapping patterns to handlers, a navigator that realizes a route into UI, and an auth/precondition gate. The single-funnel principle matters: a tap from a notification, a universal link from Safari, and an in-app button should all produce the same route so behavior is consistent and testable. Universal links require the AASA file served over HTTPS at the well-known path and the associated-domains entitlement; they open the app directly without a Safari bounce, unlike custom schemes which any app can claim. Routing must handle deferred deep links (link tapped before the user is logged in or before content has loaded) by stashing the intended route and replaying it after the precondition is met. Building the navigation stack from a cold start is the hard part — the router may need to construct intermediate screens so back navigation makes sense. Failure handling: unknown paths route to a sensible default, malformed params are rejected, and version-skew links degrade gracefully. Testability: parsing URL -> route is pure and easily unit-tested. Interviewers probe deferred deep links and consistent handling across all entry points.
f: Trap: "Custom URL schemes are just as good as universal links." Custom schemes can be hijacked by any app registering the same scheme and don't work as web links; universal links are domain-verified via the AASA file and open your app securely from real https URLs.

q: Design an A/B testing framework.
a: The core is deterministic assignment: hash a stable user or install ID together with the experiment key into a bucket so a user always lands in the same variant. The client fetches experiment definitions from remote config, evaluates eligibility and assignment locally, and exposes a typed accessor for which variant a feature should use. Crucially, an exposure event fires only when the variant is actually read at the decision point, and the analytics pipeline ties those exposures to outcome metrics. Built-in defaults guarantee a sane experience offline or before config loads.
d: Components: an experiment registry (key, variants, allocation, targeting), a deterministic assigner, an exposure logger, and an analysis pipeline (usually server-side). Deterministic bucketing via hash(unitID + experimentKey) ensures stability across sessions and devices and independence between experiments. Targeting/eligibility (app version, locale, cohort) filters who can enter; allocation splits eligible users across variants and a holdout. Exposure logging must occur at the point of use, not at assignment — otherwise you count users who never reached the feature, biasing results. Mutual exclusion groups prevent overlapping experiments from confounding each other. Statistical rigor lives server-side: sample-size/power, guardrail metrics, and sequential-testing corrections, but the client must emit clean, deduped exposure and metric events. Failure handling: offline defaults to control, config validation, and a kill switch to end a harmful experiment instantly. Edge cases: users switching between logged-out and logged-in IDs (assignment can flip), and mid-experiment allocation changes. Testability: force assignment via overrides and assert exposure firing. Interviewers probe exposure-at-decision-point and deterministic assignment.
f: Trap: "Log the user into the experiment when you assign them at fetch time." That counts users who never hit the code path, diluting the effect and biasing results; log exposure only when the variant is actually evaluated at the decision point.

q: Design a rate limiter / throttler for API calls.
a: Client-side I'd implement a token-bucket limiter: tokens refill at a steady rate up to a capacity, each request consumes a token, and requests wait or get rejected when the bucket is empty. This smooths bursts while allowing short spikes up to the bucket size. I'd combine it with respecting server signals — honoring `Retry-After` and 429 responses with backoff — since the server is the real authority. The limiter is an actor so concurrent callers coordinate safely without races.
d: Algorithms to weigh: token bucket (allows bursts up to capacity, smooth average) vs leaky bucket (strictly smooth output, no bursts) vs fixed/sliding window counters (simpler but edge-spike prone at window boundaries). Token bucket is the usual client choice for its burst tolerance. Components: a bucket holding tokens with a last-refill timestamp, an acquire() that lazily refills based on elapsed time and either grants, queues, or rejects, and per-endpoint buckets if limits differ by route. Concurrency is the trap — refill and consume must be atomic, so model the limiter as an actor or guard with a lock. Client-side limiting is cooperative and best-effort; the server enforces the real limit, so always handle 429 + `Retry-After` and apply exponential backoff with jitter to avoid synchronized retries (thundering herd). Edge cases: clock changes affecting refill math (use a monotonic clock), priority requests that should bypass, and bounded queue length to avoid unbounded memory. Testability: inject a monotonic clock to advance time deterministically. Interviewers probe token-bucket vs window correctness at boundaries and atomic refill under concurrency.
f: Trap: "A fixed-window counter (N requests per minute) is sufficient." Two bursts straddling the window boundary can send up to 2N requests in a moment, violating the intended rate; use a sliding window or token bucket to bound the true rate.

q: Design an image upload pipeline (compression, retry, background).
a: When the user picks an image I downsize and re-encode it to a target dimension and quality off the main thread, write the compressed payload to a temp file, and enqueue an upload backed by a background `URLSession` so it survives app suspension. Each upload is persisted with metadata and an idempotency key so we can retry with backoff or resume after a kill without duplicating. The UI optimistically shows the local image with a 'uploading' state and reconciles to the server URL on success. Large uploads use multipart or chunked transfer with per-chunk retry.
d: Components: a processing stage (downsample + compress, strip or preserve EXIF per privacy needs), a durable upload queue, a background-session uploader, and a state/reconciliation layer. Compression decisions trade quality vs size — downsample to display needs, choose HEIC/JPEG and a quality factor, and do it off-main to avoid jank; uploading from a file (not in-memory `Data`) is required for background sessions. Idempotency keys let the server dedupe retries, essential because background uploads can complete after the app thinks they failed. Resumable/chunked uploads matter for large files on flaky networks — upload in parts, track which chunks succeeded, and retry only the missing ones. Persistence is mandatory: the app may be killed mid-upload, so store enough to rebuild and reattach to the background session on relaunch. Failure handling: backoff with jitter, max attempts then surface an error, and validation/checksum to detect corruption. Edge cases: low disk for temp files, EXIF/location stripping for privacy, and orientation normalization. Testability: abstract the session and the compressor. Interviewers probe background-session file uploads and idempotent retries.
f: Trap: "Use uploadTask with in-memory Data for the body in a background session." Background sessions require a file URL (or stream) because the system performs the transfer out-of-process after suspension; in-memory bodies aren't supported there, so write to a temp file and upload from it.

q: Design a token-bucket request scheduler.
a: I'd model a bucket that refills tokens at a fixed rate up to a maximum capacity; each scheduled request must acquire a token to run, and if none are available it waits until the next refill. The scheduler is an actor that lazily computes refills from elapsed time on each acquire, so we don't need a timer ticking. Requests can carry priority so high-priority work jumps the queue when tokens free up. This gives smooth average throughput while tolerating short bursts up to the bucket capacity.
d: Core state: current token count, capacity, refill rate, and a last-refill monotonic timestamp. On acquire, compute elapsed time, add rate*elapsed tokens capped at capacity, then either consume one and proceed or suspend the caller until enough tokens accrue. Lazy refill (compute-on-demand) beats a background timer — fewer wakeups, better battery. Concurrency safety is the whole point: make it an actor or serialize with a lock so two callers can't both consume the last token. A priority queue of waiters lets you serve important requests first; otherwise FIFO is fairest. Use a monotonic clock so system time changes don't grant or starve tokens. Edge cases: a single request that needs more tokens than capacity (must special-case or it deadlocks), cancellation while waiting (remove from the queue and resume its continuation as cancelled), and bounding the waiter queue to avoid memory blowup. This pairs with retry/backoff for server-side 429s. Testability: inject the clock and advance it to assert pacing deterministically. Interviewers probe lazy vs timer refill and atomic consumption under concurrency.
f: Trap: "Run a repeating timer that adds a token every interval." A constantly firing timer wastes energy and drifts; compute refills lazily from elapsed monotonic time on each acquire so the scheduler is precise and wakes nothing when idle.

q: Design a local notification scheduling system.
a: I'd wrap `UNUserNotificationCenter` behind a scheduling service that takes a domain-level notification request, builds the content and a trigger (calendar, interval, or location), and registers it with a stable identifier so it can be updated or cancelled. Because iOS caps pending local notifications at 64, the service prioritizes and rolls a window of the soonest notifications rather than scheduling everything. It reconciles scheduled state against the desired set on app launch and after data changes, and handles authorization and the user disabling notifications. Deep links from a tap route through the central app router.
d: Components: an authorization manager, a request builder (content + trigger), a scheduler that diffs desired-vs-pending, and a delegate for foreground presentation and tap handling. The 64-pending limit is the central design constraint — for recurring or numerous reminders you can't just schedule them all; you maintain a rolling window of the nearest N and reschedule as time passes or on launch/background. Stable identifiers let you update or remove a notification when underlying data changes (a reminder edited or deleted should update its scheduled notification). Triggers: calendar for specific times, time-interval for relative, and location for geofenced — each with repeat semantics to consider. Reconciliation matters because the source of truth is your data, not the scheduled set; on launch you compute the desired set and add/remove deltas. Failure handling: denied authorization (degrade gracefully), time-zone and DST shifts for calendar triggers, and ensuring tapped notifications route consistently. Foreground presentation needs an explicit choice to show or suppress. Testability: abstract the notification center behind a protocol. Interviewers probe the 64-notification cap strategy and reconciliation with the data model.
f: Trap: "Schedule a local notification for every future reminder up front." iOS only keeps the 64 soonest pending notifications and silently drops the rest, so distant ones never fire; maintain a rolling window and reschedule as time advances.

q: Design a multi-step onboarding flow engine.
a: I'd represent onboarding as a declarative sequence of steps with a coordinator driving navigation, where each step declares its UI, completion criteria, and skip conditions. The engine evaluates which steps apply for a given user — based on flags, entitlements, or prior completion — and walks them in order, persisting progress so an interrupted onboarding resumes correctly. Steps are decoupled from each other and from navigation, communicating results back to the coordinator. Remote config can reorder, add, or A/B-test steps without an app update.
d: Components: a step model (id, view factory, eligibility predicate, completion handler), a flow definition (ordered, possibly remote-driven), a coordinator/state machine, and a progress store. Decoupling steps from navigation via a coordinator keeps each screen testable and reusable and lets the flow be reordered declaratively. Eligibility predicates let you skip steps that don't apply (already granted permission, returning user), and a graph rather than a strict line supports branching (different paths by user type). Persisting progress is essential — onboarding can be interrupted by a kill or a permission prompt, so resume from the last incomplete eligible step. Remote-driving the sequence enables experimentation and fixing flow mistakes without a release, but requires safe defaults and validation when config is missing. Concurrency is light (mostly main-actor UI) but permission requests are async and may bounce the app. Failure handling: a step whose backend dependency fails should be skippable or retryable, and the engine must always have an exit (never trap the user). Edge cases: app update adding new steps for existing users, and rollback. Testability: pure eligibility/ordering logic tested without UI. Interviewers probe resumability and decoupling steps from navigation.
f: Trap: "Hardcode the step order and push each screen from the previous one." That couples steps together, breaks reordering/skip/A-B testing, and makes resume-after-interruption fragile; drive the sequence from a declarative definition through a coordinator.

q: Design a caching layer with TTL and invalidation.
a: I'd build a generic cache keyed by a stable request key, storing each entry with its value, an insertion timestamp, and a TTL, so reads check freshness before returning. On expiry I support both passive eviction (treat as miss on read) and active cleanup, plus a stale-while-revalidate mode that returns stale data immediately while refreshing in the background. Invalidation comes from TTL, explicit key/tag-based purges on mutations, and version tokens like `ETag` for conditional revalidation. Two tiers — memory and disk — give speed plus persistence across launches.
d: Components: an entry wrapper (value + timestamp + TTL + optional `ETag`), a memory tier (`NSCache` for cost-based eviction), a disk tier, and an invalidation manager. TTL handles time-based staleness, but real systems need event-based invalidation too — when a user edits a resource you must purge or update related keys, often via tag/group keys so one mutation invalidates a set. Stale-while-revalidate improves perceived performance: serve stale, refresh async, swap in fresh — but you must guard against a thundering herd of revalidations by coalescing concurrent refreshes for the same key (single-flight). Conditional requests with `ETag`/`If-None-Match` let the server return 304 to extend freshness cheaply. Concurrency: reads and writes must be thread-safe (actor or lock), and eviction under memory pressure should be automatic (`NSCache` handles memory; disk needs an LRU + size cap). Failure handling: corrupt disk entries, clock changes affecting TTL (prefer monotonic or store absolute expiry), and bounded size. Edge cases: negative caching (cache 404s briefly) and cache stampede on cold start. Testability: inject the clock to test expiry. Interviewers probe invalidation beyond TTL and stampede protection.
f: Trap: "TTL expiry alone keeps the cache correct." If underlying data changes before the TTL elapses, you serve stale data for the rest of the window; pair TTL with event-driven invalidation (tag purges or `ETag` revalidation) for correctness.

q: Design a background-refresh data sync.
a: I'd register a `BGAppRefreshTask` (and a `BGProcessingTask` for heavier work) via `BGTaskScheduler`, so the system wakes the app opportunistically to fetch fresh data. The task runs a bounded sync — delta-fetch the most important data, persist it, and complete within the short budget — and always reschedules the next refresh before finishing. Because the OS decides when and whether to run it based on usage and power, I treat background refresh as best-effort and still sync on foreground. An expiration handler cleans up if the system reclaims time.
d: Components: task registration at launch, a scheduler that submits requests, a sync routine designed to be short and cancellable, and a foreground reconciliation path. iOS gives background refresh only a brief budget and runs it on its own schedule influenced by user behavior, Low Power Mode, and app usage — so it's an optimization, never a guarantee. You must register task identifiers in Info.plist and at launch, set an expiration handler (the OS calls it when time runs out — cancel work and call `setTaskCompleted` promptly or you get penalized), and reschedule the next task inside the handler since each submission runs once. Keep the work delta-based and prioritized so partial completion still helps. `BGProcessingTask` suits longer, deferrable work (often requiring charging/network conditions). Concurrency: do network and disk off the main thread and respect cancellation. Failure handling: network failure just retries next cycle; never block waiting. Edge cases: user disabling Background App Refresh entirely, and ensuring foreground sync remains the reliable path. Testability: trigger tasks via the debugger and abstract the sync logic from BGTask plumbing. Interviewers probe rescheduling, the expiration handler, and treating it as best-effort.
f: Trap: "Schedule it once and it'll keep refreshing on a fixed interval." Each `BGTaskScheduler` submission fires at most once and the OS controls timing; you must reschedule the next task from within the handler, and even then it's best-effort, not periodic.

q: Design a thread-safe in-memory store.
a: I'd model the store as an actor so all access is serialized by the compiler-enforced isolation, eliminating data races without manual locking. It exposes async read and write methods over an internal dictionary or graph, and for hot read paths I'd consider a copy-on-write snapshot so readers get a consistent immutable view without contention. Observers subscribe via an async stream that emits on changes. If actors aren't an option, I'd use a concurrent dispatch queue with barrier writes — the classic reader/writer pattern.
d: Two idiomatic designs: an actor (modern Swift concurrency, serializes all access, async API, no manual locks) or a concurrent `DispatchQueue` with `.barrier` for writes and concurrent reads (sync reads, async barrier writes — the reader/writer lock pattern). The actor approach is safest and clearest but makes every access async and introduces reentrancy/suspension subtleties. For read-heavy workloads, copy-on-write snapshots let many readers proceed lock-free against an immutable copy while writers swap in a new version, trading memory for concurrency. Change notification should be designed in: an `AsyncStream` or Combine subject emitting deltas, careful not to deliver inconsistent intermediate states. Concurrency hazards to discuss: actor reentrancy (state can change across an await), invariants spanning multiple operations (offer transactional batch methods), and avoiding accidental main-actor hops. Failure handling is mostly about consistency — never expose a partially-mutated state. Performance: lock granularity, avoiding lock convoy, and not holding isolation across long async calls. Edge cases: bulk updates, atomic compare-and-set, and bounded size with eviction. Testability: deterministic via injected behavior; stress-test with concurrent tasks under Thread Sanitizer. Interviewers probe actor reentrancy and reader/writer trade-offs.
f: Trap: "Wrapping every method body in a lock makes a multi-step update atomic." Per-method locks protect individual calls but not a read-modify-write across calls — another writer can interleave; expose a single transactional method or hold the isolation/lock across the whole compound operation.

q: Design a modular plugin architecture for a large app.
a: I'd split the app into feature modules that depend only on shared interface modules, never on each other directly, so features are independently buildable and swappable. A plugin registers itself against well-defined protocols — for routing, dependency provision, and lifecycle hooks — discovered at startup through a registry. The app shell wires modules together via dependency injection and a coordinator/router, so adding a feature means adding a module and registering it, not editing the core. This keeps build times down and ownership clear while allowing features to be enabled or A/B-tested independently.
d: Components: an interface/contract layer (protocols, shared models), feature modules (implementations), a plugin registry, a DI container, and an app shell that composes them. The key principle is dependency inversion — features depend on abstractions in a shared module, and the composition root in the shell binds implementations, so there are no feature-to-feature dependencies and the dependency graph stays acyclic. Plugins conform to extension points (routes they handle, services they expose, startup hooks) and self-register, enabling open/closed extensibility: new features plug in without modifying core. Build/scaling benefits are real: independent modules parallelize builds, reduce merge conflicts, and clarify code ownership; they also enable feature-flag gating and per-feature testability. Trade-offs: over-modularization adds boilerplate and indirection, and a too-generic plugin API becomes hard to reason about — keep contracts tight. Failure handling: a plugin failing to initialize should degrade gracefully without crashing the shell; version-skew between contract and implementation needs guarding. Routing across modules goes through the shared router so deep links resolve regardless of which module owns the screen. Testability: each module tests in isolation against contract mocks. Interviewers probe acyclic dependencies and how cross-module navigation works without coupling.
f: Trap: "Let feature modules import each other when they need to navigate between features." That recreates a tangled dependency graph, kills independent builds, and causes cycles; route cross-feature navigation through a shared router/contract layer so modules stay decoupled.

q: Design a real-time location tracking system (battery vs accuracy).
a: The central trade-off is accuracy versus battery, so I'd choose the lowest-power location API that meets the use case — significant-location-change or region monitoring for coarse needs, and `CLLocationManager` with continuous high-accuracy updates only when actively navigating. I'd adapt accuracy and update frequency dynamically based on context: high precision while in foreground navigation, dropping to deferred or significant-change updates in the background. Locations are buffered and uploaded in batches to avoid waking the radio per fix, and the app uses background location modes with the right authorization. Filtering smooths noisy GPS and discards low-confidence fixes.
d: Components: a location provider abstracting `CLLocationManager`, an accuracy/power policy engine, a buffering/upload layer, and a permission manager. The dominant design axis is energy: continuous GPS at best accuracy drains battery fast, so use the coarsest mode acceptable — `startMonitoringSignificantLocationChanges` (cell/Wi-Fi based, very low power, wakes app on big moves), region/geofence monitoring for entering/leaving areas, or full updates with `desiredAccuracy` and `distanceFilter` tuned per state. Adaptive policy: raise accuracy during active navigation, lower it when stationary or backgrounded; consider activity type and motion (Core Motion) to gate updates. Background tracking requires Always authorization, the location background mode, and on iOS a visible indicator; respect the user's choice and provisional/when-in-use states. Batching uploads (and using deferred updates) avoids constant radio wakeups — the radio is the real battery cost. Failure handling: GPS drift/outliers filtered by horizontal accuracy and speed sanity checks, signal loss, and permission downgrades at runtime. Edge cases: clock/location spoofing, indoor inaccuracy, and time-zone/coordinate edge wrapping. Testability: inject the location provider and replay recorded tracks. Interviewers probe choosing the minimal-power API and adaptive accuracy.
f: Trap: "Set desiredAccuracy to best and stream every fix to the server in real time." Continuous best-accuracy GPS plus per-fix radio wakeups crushes battery; match accuracy to the actual need, adapt by context, and batch uploads to keep the radio asleep.

q: Design a real-time connection manager (WebSocket lifecycle, reconnection, heartbeat, presence).
a: I'd centralize a single persistent connection behind a manager that owns the socket lifecycle and exposes a stream of typed inbound events plus a send API. It maintains a heartbeat ping/pong to detect dead connections the OS hasn't torn down yet, and on any failure it reconnects with exponential backoff and jitter, resubscribing to channels and replaying queued sends afterward. Connection state is driven by app lifecycle and network reachability — disconnect on background, reconnect and resync on foreground or when the network returns. Presence is published as a periodic signal and reconciled by the server with a timeout.
d: Components: a state machine (disconnected/connecting/connected/reconnecting/suspended), the transport (`URLSessionWebSocketTask`), a heartbeat timer, a send queue, and a subscription registry. The state machine is the backbone — every transition (network drop, background, server close, manual close) is explicit, preventing the classic bug of overlapping or zombie connections. Heartbeats matter because TCP can stay half-open silently; a ping with a pong-timeout detects a dead link faster than waiting for a write to fail, and missing N pongs forces a reconnect. Reconnection uses exponential backoff with jitter and a cap to avoid hammering the server during an outage (thundering herd across many clients), and on reconnect you must re-establish subscriptions and replay any queued outbound messages with idempotency keys. Lifecycle integration: tear down on background to respect OS limits, observe reachability to reconnect proactively, and resync missed state on return (often a sequence cursor like the chat case). Concurrency: serialize socket access (an actor) since send/receive/reconnect can race. Failure handling: distinguish recoverable (network) from fatal (auth rejected -> stop and surface) closes. Testability: abstract the transport so you can simulate drops and pong timeouts. Interviewers probe heartbeat-based dead-connection detection and backoff with jitter.
f: Trap: "If the socket is still 'connected', messages are getting through." TCP connections can go half-open and report connected while silently dropping data; rely on an application-level heartbeat with pong timeouts to detect a dead link, not the socket's own status.
